pred: In a significant development, North and South Korea have agreed to march together under one flag at the upcoming Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang, marking a rare moment of unity between the two nations. This comes after a series of talks between the two countries, which have been frozen for over two years, aimed at improving their relations. The two Koreas will also field a joint women's ice hockey team and compete together in other sports events, including skiing and cultural events. North Korea will send a delegation of around 40 athletes, including 23 cheerleaders, 140 artists, and 30 Taekwondo performers, and will also send a separate delegation of around 150 athletes to the Paralympics. The move is seen as a diplomatic breakthrough, but there are concerns that Pyongyang may be using the talks to buy time to pursue its weapons program. The International Olympics Committee (IOC) has approved the joint hockey team proposal, but the details of the agreement are still being worked out. The thaw in relations between North and South Korea has been met with mixed reactions, with some athletes and officials expressing reservations about competing together, while others see it as a positive step towards reconciliation. Japan has warned against being too naive about North Korea's "charm offensive" and China has criticized the talks as showing a "Cold War mentality." The two Koreas are still technically at war, as the 1950-53 conflict ended in a truce rather than a peace treaty, but the recent developments have raised hopes for improved ties.
answers: North and South Korea will compete together under one unified flag at next month's Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang, in what will be the first such arrangement since 2006. South Korea previously announced North Korea would send officials, athletes, and a cheer squad to the Olympics following talks between the two countries. It has now revealed North Korea's intention to send a 550-member delegation. Among those arriving in South Korea beginning on Jan. 25 will be female ice hockey players joining a team of South Korean athletes (they'll compete under a flag showing the Korean Peninsula in blue on a white background), as well as 230 cheerleaders, 140 orchestra members, and 30 people who will take part in a taekwondo demonstration, report Sky News and CNN. Ahead of the Olympics, skiers from both countries will also train together in North Korea, per CNN. The thawing of relations between the North and South has drawn praise from some. But others, like Japanese Foreign Minister Taro Kono, fear Kim Jong Un only hopes to ease international pressure while continuing to develop a nuclear weapons arsenal. "It is not the time to ease pressure, or to reward North Korea," Kono said Tuesday in Vancouver, Canada, where 20 countries met and agreed to consider tougher sanctions on the North, per Reuters. Warning countries not to be fooled by its "charm offensive," Kono added, "The fact that North Korea is engaging in dialogue could be interpreted as proof that the sanctions are working." China avoided the meeting, saying it showed a "Cold War mentality."

answers: Kanye West is, apparently, $53 million in debt, but he should be able to pay that off in no time thanks to a GoFundMe campaign started by 36-year-old Minnesotan Jeremy Piatt, CNET reports. pred: A Chinese man in Gansu province has won over $80,000 in compensation after being attacked by a wild panda last year. Guan Quanzhi, a farmer in Li Baizi village, was working on his land when the panda suddenly appeared and bit his leg, requiring seven hours of surgery. He sued local government officials and the nearby Baishuijiang National Reserve, resulting in a settlement of 52,000 yuan ($83,000). This incident highlights the danger posed by wild pandas, despite their cuddly appearance, as they are members of the bear family and can inflict serious injury with their powerful jaws and teeth. In recent years, the number of wild pandas has increased by nearly 17%, according to government data, likely due to conservation efforts. However, the animals continue to face threats to their habitat and have been known to attack humans, including a 2008 incident where a panda mauled a 20-year-old man at a zoo. The World Wildlife Fund warns that while they primarily feed on bamboo, pandas can be dangerous when provoked. Meanwhile, pandas are a popular tourist attraction in China, with the government profiting from renting them to foreign zoos. A villager in a separate incident was compensated 52,000 yuan after his leg may require amputation following a panda attack.
answers: Pandas may prefer their meals in the form of bamboo, but that doesn't mean their bite is toothless. A man in China recently received a harsh reminder of that after a wild panda bit his leg after authorities pursued the critter onto his lawn, reports AFP via Yahoo. The creature had made its way into a northwestern village, which is near a sanctuary that's home to about 100 wild pandas. "I saw a panda jump out in front of me, its body completely covered in mud," the man told local news. A struggle ensued, in which the bear wouldn't release the man's leg until a rescuer wrapped a coat around the animal's head. Finally, it fled—but not before causing injuries which, China Daily reports, led to eight surgeries over the past year and may lead to amputation. The man sued three government departments and won an $83,000 payout that will cover his medical costs, his lawyer says. (China Daily has a pretty gruesome photo of the injury.) "As cuddly as they may look, a panda can protect itself as well as most other bears," the World Wildlife Fund notes, per AFP. Indeed, panda attacks have led to some nasty consequences in the past.

pred: Apple's recent product launch event sparked a heated debate among tech enthusiasts and critics, with many expressing frustration over the company's decision to eliminate the headphone jack from the new iPhone 7 and iPhone 7 Plus. Many have pointed out that the move is not justified by a lack of technical excuse and that it goes against the company's previous mantra of "it just works" and compatibility with any headphones. The marketing chief, Phil Schiller, was mocked on Twitter for his explanation that the decision was made out of "courage" to move forward and do something better for everyone. Critics argue that courage involves doing something that is difficult or scary, not eradicating a widely-used and successful standard for proprietary technology. The removal of the headphone jack has been compared to San Francisco's treatment of the middle class and the construction of a wall beyond the dreams of Trump, with users now having to pay for expensive and unwieldy dongles to use their headphones. Some have also questioned the logic of prioritizing stereo speakers and Taptic engines over the ability to use any headphones they want. The event has been described as a step backward for users, with the only upside being the ability to charge and listen to music wirelessly with Apple's AirPods, which have been criticized for their design and cost. Overall, the reaction to the move has been one of disappointment and frustration at Apple's decision to prioritize its own proprietary technology over user convenience and flexibility.
answers: Apple presented its new smartphone Wednesday, and nestled in with the iPhone 7's much-ballyhooed features was the disclosure that the company is getting rid of the phone's headphone jack—news that wasn't exactly met with universal acclaim, or much acclaim at all. Chris Taylor is one detractor of the headphone ditch, writing for Mashable that, in nearly 20 years of attending Apple product launches, he's "never heard anything as ridiculous emanate from that stage as I did Wednesday." Apple marketing head Phil Schiller used the word "courage" to describe Apple's innovation, but that's not how Taylor sees it. "Courage is marching across the Edmund Pettus bridge in Selma in 1965 [or] facing down a tank in Tiananmen Square," he writes. "Courage, by definition, involves doing something that makes you afraid." Apparently Taylor doesn't think Apple was quivering in its workboots when it came up with this plan. He's got plenty of other words instead of "courage" in mind, including "hubris," "arrogance," "greed," and his personal favorite, "stupidity," which he attributes to Apple for taking "one step forward, two steps back" both technologically ("wired almost always sounds better than wireless") and logistically (no more plugging in other peripherals like credit card readers, for example). Meanwhile, although much of the other feedback online is similarly cranky about the headphone jack's demise, Quartz notes that Apple is actually late, not early, in making this move: A handful of Chinese smartphone brands have been fiddling around with the idea for years, and some have already sent these phones, sans jacks, to market.

pred: Hurricane Irene is expected to make landfall in the eastern United States this weekend, with the potential to cause widespread damage and flooding along the coastal areas of North Carolina, Virginia, Maryland, Delaware, New Jersey, and New York. States of emergency have been declared in several states, and mandatory evacuations have been issued for some areas. The storm is forecasted to be a Category 3 hurricane with winds of up to 115 mph and could potentially strengthen into a Category 4, causing $13.9 billion in insured losses and $20 billion in economic losses. The National Hurricane Center warns of tidal surges of 5-10 feet and destructive waves, and forecasts indicate the storm will make landfall along the North Carolina Outer Banks on Saturday. The memorial dedication of the Martin Luther King Jr. memorial on the National Mall has been postponed. Amtrak and airlines have canceled some flights and trains, and Metro stations are preparing for power outages. New York City officials are planning to shut down the mass transit system on Saturday. Residents are advised to stock up on supplies and prepare for extended power outages, and the Navy has moved ships away from the coast to avoid damage. In the Bahamas, the storm has already caused damage, flooding, and power outages. The storm is expected to be slow-moving, bringing heavy rain and winds to the area for an extended period of time.
answers: Frightened North Carolinians fleeing Irene are jamming highways as the East Coast steels for what could be the biggest hurricane in decades. At least seven states have now declared a state of emergency, and hundreds of flights are being canceled. Gas stations are running out of fuel and ATMs have been emptied of cash as Americans hit the road for a bumper-to-bumper trip out of Irene's angry path. The Category 3 storm is expected to hit the Southeast sometime tomorrow, and hundreds of thousands of residents have been ordered out of their homes in three North Carolina counties. Norfolk, Va., is also ordering residents to evacuate, and Washington, DC, has been forced to postpone Sunday's planned MLK Memorial dedication. "This is a very, very serious situation," a spokeswoman for Dare County told ABC News. "We have not seen anything like this in the lifetimes of most our residents. Once the storm hits, it will be very difficult to respond to distress calls." Irene is some 700 miles wide now and moving at a slow 12mph, which means it can wreak extensive damage in a region over a long period of time; it could cause up to $13.9 billion in damage on the East Coast. The storm is expected to barrel into New York City on Sunday, packing winds of up to 90mph. New Jersey communities are already being evacuated, and hundreds of street fairs have been canceled and elder care facilities and hospitals in low-lying area will be evacuated in New York today. NYC Mayor Michael Bloomberg says residents in low-lying parts of the city should get out today as the transit system is planned to be shut down tomorrow.

pred:
CBS News is undergoing a major revamp of its morning show "The Early Show" with significant changes to the on-air team. The current co-hosts, Harry Smith and Maggie Rodriguez, will be leaving the show, and will be replaced by Chris Wragge and Erica Hill, who have been anchoring the Saturday edition of the program since 2008. Jeff Glor, who has been anchoring the Saturday newscast, and Marysol Castro, formerly of ABC's "Good Morning America" weekend edition, will join the team as weather anchor. This comes after the show's executive producer, David Friedman, changed within the past year. The new team is expected to start on January 3rd. The move is seen as an attempt to freshen up the show and compete with NBC's "Today" show and ABC's "Good Morning America," which have consistently ranked higher in ratings. Smith, who has been with the show for 17 years, will become a primary substitute anchor for "CBS Evening News" and "Face the Nation," while Rodriguez and Price's future roles are still to be determined. The suddenness of the changes has raised questions about desperation, but CBS News President Sean McManus believes the new team has the right energy and chemistry to move the show forward. The new team has already proven its ability to work well together on Saturday mornings, according to McManus.


In other news, the old adage "the early bird gets the worm" does not seem to apply to CBS' "Early Show," as the program is undergoing another round of musical chairs by replacing its on-air team. Maggie Rodriguez, Harry Smith, and Dave Price are exiting, while Chris Wragge and Erica Hill, who have been co-anchoring the Saturday edition since 2008, will take over as co-anchors of the weekday show starting January 3rd. Jeff Glor, who has been anchoring the Saturday newscast, and Marysol Castro, from ABC's "Good Morning America" weekend edition, will join the team as weather anchor. While Smith will become a primary substitute for Katie Couric's evening newscasts, Rodriguez and Price's future roles are still to be determined. The
answers: CBS is scrapping its Early Show team, replacing co-hosts Harry Smith and Maggie Rodriguez with the anchors of the show's Saturday edition, Chris Wragge and Erica Hill. Other more minor players also are leaving in the switch, which takes place early next year, the LA Times reports. The show has been consistently stuck in third place, well behind Today and Good Morning America. Smith, who survived the last major overhaul of the show in 2002, will stick with the network as Katie Couric's main backup on the CBS Evening News, notes AP. Click here for more.

pred: In Detroit, Michigan, the Detroit Water and Sewerage Department (DWSD) has been implementing a plan to shut off water service to tens of thousands of non-paying customers, leading to protests and civil disobedience from activists who argue that it is a violation of human rights. The DWSD has been shutting off water to residential customers who are at least two months behind on their payments, including families with young children and the elderly, while ignoring debts from larger entities such as golf courses and the state of Michigan. Despite the department's claims that it needs to recoup $175 million in outstanding bills, it has announced a $1 million fund to help low-income residents avoid shutoffs. However, activists say that this is not enough and that many others will likely lose access to water over the summer if the shutoffs continue. The DWSD has also begun intensifying efforts to collect unpaid debts from commercial customers, with 12% of accounts being delinquent. The department has released a list of the top 40 commercial and industrial accounts with the highest balances, including Vargo Golf, which owes $43,714, and the State of Michigan, which owes $70,26. Activists have appealed to the United Nations for help, and a coalition of welfare rights organizations has called for the water service to be restored to customers and for the shutoffs to stop. Meanwhile, police have arrested several protesters, including religious leaders, for disorderly conduct. The issue has garnered national attention and a Detroit congressman has called for federal action to address the problem.
answers: At least eight people were arrested yesterday in Detroit for protesting water shutoffs in a city where almost 40% of residents live below the poverty line. Dozens picketed in front of a facility affiliated with the city's Water and Sewerage Department to try to stop what activists call a “human rights violation,” reports Al Jazeera America. Police tell MLive that the individuals charged with misdemeanor disorderly conduct range in age from 58 to 70 and include members of the clergy. (Watch video of the protest and arrests here.) The Rev. Bill Wylie-Kellermann, one of the arrested protesters, tells the Detroit Free Press that police tried to "move us forcibly, and we sat down. … We [were] here to appeal to the workers to stop shutting off the water." The coordinator of a local activist group says "police officers should be arresting rapists and murderers instead of arresting peaceful people." A Detroit Police rep counters that protesters still have to follow rules: "You cannot impede pedestrian or vehicle traffic." The Free Press counts 7,210 shutoffs in June, up from 7,556 in April and May combined, as part of what Al Jazeera reports is an effort to recover $175 million in unpaid bills. As of March, nearly half of the city's 323,900 DWSD accounts were delinquent; activists have asked the UN to intervene.

pred: On October 3, 1995, O.J. Simpson was acquitted of the murders of his ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald Goldman, but he was later convicted of unrelated crimes in 2007 and is currently serving a nine to 3-year sentence in Nevada prison. He will appear before a parole board on Thursday to make his case for release, which could potentially happen as early as October 1. Simpson's net worth is estimated to be around $25,000, which is a fraction of the $10.8 million he had when he and Nicole Brown Simpson divorced in 1992, and he has been struggling financially since the Nicole Brown trial. Despite being acquitted in the famous criminal case, Simpson was found guilty in a civil suit and ordered to pay $3.5 million to the Brown and Goldman families, and he defaulted on his Brentwood home mortgage. Simpson's football and acting career were also highlighted, including his Heisman Trophy win, NFL Hall of Fame induction, and roles in films and TV shows. The parole hearing is expected to be a spectacle, and Simpson's friend Tom Scotto said he wants to disappear after his release and not seek attention. The board will consider Simpson's behavior in prison and a risk assessment, and Simpson has expressed remorse for the 2007 robbery. Simpson has been incarcerated since December 2008 and has missed important events in his children's lives.
answers: OJ Simpson's parole hearing in Nevada will be broadcast live at 1pm EDT Thursday and there's a strong chance that his path to freedom will be clear by the end of the afternoon. Nobody has registered to testify against the 70-year-old Simpson, who has been in prison for nine years on 12 convictions related to a Las Vegas robbery and will appear before the Nevada Parole Board via videolink, NPR reports. Thursday's hearing will cover four concurrent sentences for use of a deadly weapon and two consecutive sentences for assault with a deadly weapon, the Las Vegas Review-Journal reports. Four board members will vote after the hearing and Simpson could be free by Oct. 1 if they are all in favor of parole. If Simpson is indeed released this fall—which appears almost certainly to be the decision—it will "renew the rabid curiosity" in the case and "produce a spectacle unlike anything we’ve seen since he went behind bars," Nancy Armour at USA Today predicts. There has even been talk that Simpson might end up with a reality TV show, but Armour wants Simpson to avoid the spotlight unless he wants to return to the "toxic mentality that landed him in prison in the first place." If the former NFL star does end up with a TV show, the money is likely to go to the families of murder victims Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald Goldman, CBS News reports. He will still receive an NFL pension estimated at $25,000 per month.

pred: Google CEO Sundar Pichai has been awarded $19 million worth of shares in Alphabet Inc., making him one of the highest-paid executives in the US. This grant vests in quarterly increments over the next four years and brings his holdings in the company to $65 million. While his stake is still significantly lower than that of the company's founders, Larry Page and Sergey Brin, it is more than the $37 million awarded to Apple CEO Tim Cook. Pichai celebrated his award by watching the Super Bowl and meeting NFL legend Ronnie Lott. Additionally, other Google executives, including CFO Ruth Porat and cloud computing head Diane Greene, were also granted significant amounts of shares. The award comes as Google has been under scrutiny for its tax deal with the UK government, with critics calculating that the company generated £24 billion in sales in the country between 2005 and 2014 and paid only £70 million in taxes, equating to an effective tax rate of 2-3%.
answers: Google's parent company appears to be satisfied with how Sundar Pichai has performed during his four months as Google CEO, judging by the gargantuan $199 million equity award revealed in a securities filing. Alphabet stated that Pichai was awarded 273,328 Class C Google stock units on Feb. 3, bringing his holdings to around $650 million, reports the Wall Street Journal. The award makes Pichai the highest-paid CEO in America, according to the Guardian, which notes that Pichai's fortune is dwarfed by those of Google founders Larry Page and Sergey Brin, who are worth around $34 billion each, and by that of previous CEO Eric Schmidt, who's sitting on around $3 billion. (A former Google employee was awarded exactly $6,006.13.)

pred: AirAsia Flight 8501, a flight from Surabaya to Singapore, crashed into the Java Sea on December 28th, 2014, with 162 people on board. The plane was not authorized to fly on Sundays, but it took off on that day, leading to an investigation into the airline's schedules and licence. Search teams have found four large parts of the plane, including the main fuselage, and an oil slick, which suggests that extreme weather may have caused the crash. Divers are expected to explore the wreckage on Sunday, weather permitting. So far, only 30 bodies have been found, but the discovery of the main fuselage and other debris could help investigators determine the cause of the crash. The airline, AirAsia, has been grounded by Indonesian authorities, and the Transport Ministry is reviewing all of its flights. The crash is the first for the airline, which began operations in 20014 and became popular in the region. The cause of the crash remains unexplained, but weather conditions and mechanical error are being investigated. Family members of the victims are waiting for news of their loved ones, with some hoping that the discovery of the plane will bring closure.
answers: Search teams in Indonesia may have found the main fuselage of the AirAsia plane where most of the remaining victims and the plane's black boxes are expected to be. Authorities found "four big parts of the plane we're looking for," says the official in charge of the search in the Java Sea, reports Reuters. The biggest of the pieces is about 59 feet long and 18 feet wide, reports AP. The pieces have only been detected, not recovered, and divers hope conditions will permit them to reach the objects tomorrow. So far, only 30 of 162 bodies have been recovered, and most of the victims are believed to be still strapped in their seats. Authorities also said today that AirAsia was in violation of its license by flying to Singapore on a Sunday, the day of the crash, reports the Telegraph. As a result, the airline might have its license revoked in Indonesia. There's still no conclusions yet in what caused the crash, but theories continue to center on stormy conditions. "Flight 8501 appears to have been trapped in bad weather that would have been difficult to avoid," says a report by Indonesia's Meteorology, Climatology and Geophysics Agency.

pred: Actor Tom Sizemore has been accused of sexually assaulting an 1-year-old girl on the set of the 2003 film "Born Killers" during a photo shoot. According to multiple sources, including the Hollywood Reporter, Sizemore was suspended from the production after the child's parents reported the incident, but he was later allowed to return for reshoots. The alleged assault reportedly occurred during a second-unit still portrait session, where the child was seated on Sizemore's lap and he allegedly touched her genitals. The incident has never been publicly revealed until now, but it has inspired a dozen cast and crew members to come forward and share their accounts of the event. Sizemore has a history of drug use and battery against women, and has previously been convicted of physically abusing his ex-girlfriend, the former "Hollywood Madam" Heidi Fleiss. Despite his denial of the allegations, his management firm and talent agency dropped him shortly after the incident. The producers of the film removed Sizemore from the set and encouraged the child's parents to contact the police, but they declined to press charges. Sizemore has continued to work steadily in the industry, including in recent TV shows and films, despite his reputation and past legal issues. The incident has sparked a renewed conversation about sexual harassment and assault in Hollywood, with many calling for industry transparency and accountability.
answers: The latest disturbing Hollywood sexual misconduct allegation involves Tom Sizemore, an actor who has already faced drug and domestic violence charges. Multiple cast and crew members tell the Hollywood Reporter that Sizemore, best known for roles in war films including Black Hawk Down, was kicked off a Utah movie set in 2003 for allegedly touching an 11-year-old girl's genitals. They say the day after a Born Killers scene in which the girl sat on Sizemore's lap, she told her parents that he had touched her inappropriately, possibly putting his finger inside her. "At one point her eyes got just huge, like she could've vomited. I was watching her," co-star Robyn Adamson says. "Later, when I was told about what happened, I knew exactly what it was." "There was never any doubt. He was this guy who was already known for making inappropriate comments, being drunk, being high," says production assistant Roi Maufas. "Then this happens. Guys reached for hammers," he says. Producers say Sizemore, who was ordered to leave the set, denied the allegations. Crew members say the girl's parents spoke to police, but ended up not pressing charges, possibly out of fear of ruining her film career. She is now 26 and says she is considering legal action against both Sizemore and her parents. Sizemore, 55, was dropped by his management firm soon after the incident. Former manager Charles Lago tells USA Today that he is "not surprised" by the allegations. "He's the most abhorrent person I've ever met in my life," Lago says.

pred: The latest news covers two separate legal issues related to the concept of habeas corpus, a fundamental legal principle that ensures individuals are not detained indefinitely without cause. In the first passage, the article discusses how the Military Commissions Act of 2006 has been used to revoke the right to habeas corpus for detainees at Guantanamo Bay and elsewhere, allowing the government to detain individuals without providing evidence or legal recourse. This has resulted in nearly 80 men being held at Guantanamo for years without being charged with any crimes, despite the right to challenge their detention. The Supreme Court is set to hear a case challenging the detention of these detainees for the third time. In the second passage, a court has granted two chimpanzees at Stony Brook University the right to a writ of habeas corpus, allowing them to challenge their detention in court, marking the first time an animal has been covered by the legal principle. The case could potentially lead to the recognition of chimpanzees as legal persons and the release of the animals from captivity. However, some legal experts caution that the ruling may not necessarily mean that chimpanzees are considered legal persons and that the case may be part of a larger strategy to gather evidence for future arguments.
answers: The writ of habeas corpus lets prisoners appear in court to make their captors justify why they're being held. Until now in the US, those who've used this legal tactic have been human. But a New York judge yesterday ruled that the writ may be used by two lab chimpanzees at Stony Brook University, Science magazine reports. NY Supreme Court Justice Barbara Jaffe ordered a university rep to address a suit filed by the Nonhuman Rights Project by appearing in court May 6 to argue why the college is holding Hercules and Leo. The group, which says the animals are being detained for biomedical experiments, says its ultimate goal is to have the animals released to the Save the Chimps sanctuary in Fort Pierce, Fla., per the NhRP's website. "This is a big step forward to getting what we are ultimately seeking: the right to bodily liberty for chimpanzees and other cognitively complex animals," Natalie Prosin, the project's executive director, tells Science. The case kicked off in December 2013 with lawsuits that also included two chimps held on private property; each case was struck down, sending the NhRP down the appeals path. Not everyone's convinced the upcoming hearing will mean liberation for the chimps. A Pepperdine University law professor tells Science that the judge likely just wants to hear both sides of the story: "It would be quite surprising if the judge intended to make a momentous substantive finding that chimpanzees are legal persons" without doing so, he notes. Prosin, meanwhile, says her group isn't stopping with the chimps and will also target the release of other animals. (Click to read about what chimps talk to each other about.)

pred: Donald Trump has been critical of the media in recent days, accusing them of unfairly covering his campaign and not showing the true size of his rallies. In a series of tweets, he claimed that if the media reported on him honestly, he would be leading Hillary Clinton by 20%. He also blamed the media for his struggling campaign, saying they are putting "false meaning into the words I say." Trump's relationship with the media has been shaky, with him denying credentials to some reporters and accusing them of being dishonest. In a campaign rally, he called the media "disgusting" and "corrupt." Trump's running mate, Mike Pence, said the campaign will offer "real specifics" on how to make the country safer this week. Meanwhile, a New York Times investigation found that Trump's campaign chairman, Paul Manafort, has ties to a Ukrainian political party that was used to loot assets and influence elections. The article was shared by former Trump campaign manager Corey Lewandowski, who was fired by Manafort, leading to awkward social media reactions. The majority of Americans believe Russian hacking did not sway the election, according to a new poll.
answers: Donald Trump's campaign chief may have closer—and more illegal—ties to pro-Russia interests in Ukraine than he has let on, according to a New York Times report on secret ledgers that anti-corruption investigators have uncovered in Kiev. Investigators say the ledgers list $12.7 million in payments to Paul Manafort from the pro-Russia Party of Regions between 2007 and 2012, when he worked as a consultant for the party. It isn't clear whether Manafort actually received the cash, which investigators say is linked to a network that also bribed election officials. Manafort's lawyer says his client never received the payments and any suspicions are "probably heavily politically tinged." In other coverage: The Hill reports that there doesn't appear to be any love lost between Manafort and former Trump campaign manager Corey Lewandowski: Lewandowski, who was fired after clashing with Manafort, tweeted a link to the NYT Ukraine story minutes after it first appeared. The AP reports that Trump plans to deliver a major foreign policy speech in Ohio Monday that will focus on "realism," with policies including destroying ISIS without engaging in "nation-building"—and on new ideological tests for people seeking to enter the US. Politico reports that Trump targeted the media in a series of tweets Sunday, claiming that he would be beating Hillary Clinton by 20% if the "disgusting" media covered him honestly. "I am not only fighting Crooked Hillary, I am fighting the dishonest and corrupt media and her government protection process," he tweeted. "It is not 'freedom of the press' when newspapers and others are allowed to say and write whatever they want even if it is completely false!" he added. The Wall Street Journal editorial board warned Sunday that Trump's "window for a turnaround" is closing and his GOP supporters now face a "moment of truth" before he is written off as a lost cause. "The tragedy is that this is happening in a year when Republicans should win," they write.

pred: On July 13, 2015, a small plane carrying three passengers, including two step-grandparents, crashed in the rugged terrain of North Cascades, Washington. One of the passengers, 16-year-old Autumn Veatch, survived the crash and managed to hike out of the mountains and find her way to a general store in Mazama, where she was found by a motorist and taken to a hospital for treatment of minor injuries. Despite the initial fears of her being dead, Autumn's survival was confirmed, and authorities were able to narrow down the search area for the downed plane based on her cellphone data. The fate of the two step-grandparents, Leland and Sharon Bowman, remains unknown. The search for the plane and its occupants continues, with five aircraft and two Navy helicopters involved in the effort. The survival of Autumn has brought both joy and regret to her family and rescuers, who are working tirelessly to locate the wreckage and determine the fate of the other passengers. Autumn's father, David Veatch, spoke to the media at the Civil Air Patrol station in Bellingham, expressing his relief and gratitude for her safe return.
answers: A 16-year-old girl in Washington state is bruised and dehydrated but very much alive after surviving a plane crash and a long trek through what authorities describe as some of the state's most rugged terrain. Autumn Veatch was on a small plane with her step-grandparents that crashed after departing Kalispell, Mont., on Saturday afternoon, the Bellingham Herald reports. There was no sign of the plane or its occupants until a motorist found Veatch on State Route 20 yesterday afternoon and took her to a general store, where employees called authorities, the AP reports. She was hospitalized with minor injuries. Her father tells the Herald that after the plane crashed into a mountain, it caught fire and she was unable to get her step-grandparents out. She spent about a day near the plane waiting for rescue before following a stream and then a trail out of the woods, her father says. Friends say both father and daughter are completely overwhelmed. "She did joke that it was a good thing she'd watched all those Survivor shows that she didn't like, but her dad made her watch anyway," a family friend tells the Seattle Times. Authorities are still searching for the missing Beech A-35. (In Colombia last month, a mother and baby survived four days in the jungle after a plane crash.)

pred: President-elect Donald Trump has been critical of President Obama's efforts to ensure a smooth transition of power, accusing him of putting "roadblocks" in his way. Trump took to Twitter to express his frustration, saying he was "doing my best to disregard the many inflammatory President O statements and roadblocks." Despite this, Trump claimed that the transition is going "very smoothly" during a brief appearance and press conference. However, earlier in the day, he had a "nice conversation" with Obama, according to Trump, who also said that he had a "good talk" with him. The two had previously had a disagreement over the outcome of the 2016 election, with Obama claiming he could have won and Trump disagreeing. Secretary of State John Kerry is also facing criticism for the Obama administration's actions in the Middle East, particularly the failure to halt the carnage in Syria and Russia's growing influence. Kerry is set to leave office with a mixed legacy, including a historic nuclear deal and climate change treaty, but also a lack of progress on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict and Saudi Arabia's bombing of civilians in Yemen.
answers: It looks like the honeymoon is over between Trump and Obama. "Doing my best to disregard the many inflammatory President O statements and roadblocks. Thought it was going to be a smooth transition—NOT!" Trump tweeted Wednesday. The Los Angeles Times notes Trump's use of "1990s-style sarcasm." Trump had previously commended Obama on his commitment to a smooth transition of power, but a number of things have changed since then, the Washington Post reports. Many saw comments made by Obama during Tuesday's Pearl Harbor anniversary as directed at Trump. The president urged the US not to "turn inward" and to "resist the urge to demonize those who are different." Trump is also upset with the Obama administration for allowing the UN to pass a resolution condemning Israeli settlements, as well as with Obama personally for saying in an interview he could have beaten Trump and won a third term. In addition, the White House is defending the Department of Energy for not complying with a request from Trump's transition team for the names of employees involved in climate policy, ABC News reports. Transition team spokesperson Sean Spicer at first said Trump's tweets "speak for themselves, very clearly," but later complimented members of the Obama administration for being "helpful and generous with their time."

pred: On November 16, 2012, Israel authorized the mobilization of up to 75,000 reservists in preparation for a possible ground invasion of the Gaza Strip, following rocket attacks on Jerusalem and Tel Aviv. The Israeli military has been conducting airstrikes and artillery attacks on Gaza since Wednesday, killing 19 Palestinians and 3 Israelis, and Hamas has continued to fire rockets into Israel. Egypt's Prime Minister Hisham Kandil visited Gaza in an attempt to mediate a truce, but the truce did not hold. The violence has stoked fears of a wider conflict in the region, with Egypt's President Mohamed Mursi vowing to support Gaza and the United States urging friendly countries to use their influence to defuse the situation. The Israeli cabinet approved the mobilization of up to 75,000 reservists, and the military has been preparing for a possible ground operation. Palestinian officials say 30 Palestinians have been killed and 101 children and 96 women injured in the Israeli strikes. The situation remains tense, with no signs of a ceasefire in sight.
answers: A brief, planned ceasefire failed to materialize in Gaza today, as death continued to rain down on the strip. Israel had offered to hold its offensive to accommodate a visit from Egyptian Prime Minister Hesham Kandil, provided Hamas held its fire as well. But Hamas said it would keep firing, and one Israeli defense minister complained that 50 rockets had flown in from Gaza during Kandil's visit, CNN reports. A Hamas-run TV station reported that Israel had kept firing as well, but Israel says it took a break for at least two hours. answers: Rachel Borch was out for a jog in the woods near her home in Hope, Maine, when a "ferocious-looking" attacker with beady eyes and tiny teeth made a beeline for her, reports the Camden Herald. pred: In two separate incidents, a professor in Analytical Chemistry, Charlotta Turner, went above and beyond to help one of her graduate students, Firas Jumaah, who was in a dire situation in his native Iraq. answers: Four years ago, a chemistry professor got a text from her grad student: If I'm not back in a week, cut me from the doctoral program. "I could hear that the situation was hopeless and they had to flee." answers: Will Smith stars as Nicky, an established con man who takes on a new apprentice, Australian newcomer Margot Robbie's Jess, in Focus. They both love the con game, but who's playing who? answers: They volunteered to fight fires, but authorities say they were also purposely setting fires over a period of almost two years. pred: In a groundbreaking study, researchers from UC Berkeley have successfully reconstructed YouTube videos from viewers' brain activity, marking a significant step towards "reading minds." The team used functional MRI to record the amount of blood flow in the visual cortex of subjects while they watched movie trailers, and then used this data to develop a computer algorithm that could match neural patterns with the visual information in the videos. The resulting video was a blurry, dream-like interpretation of reality, with the ability to reconstruct dynamic visual experiences. According to the study's lead author, this technology could potentially be used to see into people's dreams, memories, and even fantasies. Another study from the same researchers found a way to "see through" another person's eyes by reconstructing videos based on their brain activity, with the potential to broadcast imagery from the mind. The team believes that understanding how the brain processes visual experiences is crucial for further advancements in this field, and hopes to expand their research to include more visual modules and use brain waves to control the environment. While the technology has potential applications in detective work and court cases, it also raises ethical questions about the accuracy and privacy of such readings.
answers: Scientists at UC Berkeley have made a major advancement in the field of mind reading, reconstructing YouTube videos based on brain scans from people who’d seen them. Researchers would put subjects into an MRI machine and track their brain activity as they viewed videos. Once they’d build a model of how a subject’s mind processed the video, “we could read brain activity for that subject and run it backward … to try to uncover what the viewer saw,” one study coauthor tells ABC News. Using the scans they were then able to reproduce the videos—though they’re blurry. “This is a major leap,” the co-author says. He thinks the technique could eventually be used to reconstruct dreams or memories, if it turns out the brain processes those things similarly to how it processes movies. “It’s our next line of research.” (More details on how the experiment worked, along with a video, at PC Magazine.)

pred: The latest research on dinosaurs and their vocalizations has revealed some surprising findings. According to a new study published in the journal Evolution, it is likely that dinosaurs did not roar like the iconic T. rex sound depicted in movies and TV shows, but instead made more muted sounds like coos or murmurs. The study analyzed the vocal abilities of living birds and found that large-bodied species, such as ostriches and cassowaries, are more likely to produce closed-mouth vocalizations, which are low-pitched and throaty. These sounds are produced by inflating an air sac in the neck, rather than through the open beak. The research suggests that this ability may have evolved in dinosaurs as well, particularly in species with larger bodies, and could have been used for mating displays. The study also found that the sound designers behind the original Jurassic Park movie used a mix of real and manipulated animal sounds to create the iconic dinosaur noises, including a goose for the raptor's hiss and a Jack Russell terrier for the T. rex's roar. Meanwhile, a separate study examining the distribution of closed-mouth vocalizations in birds and related groups found that this ability has evolved at least 16 times, with larger-bodied species being more likely to use it. These findings suggest that some dinosaurs may have made similar sounds to modern birds, including closed-mouth vocalizations during mating displays. However, direct fossil evidence of what dinosaurs sounded like remains elusive.
answers: Dinosaurs may have been much more like modern birds than we knew—and not just because some had feathers. A new study suggests that mighty dinosaurs of yore didn't roar, contrary to every dinosaur movie you've ever seen. Instead, they made a decidedly less scary sound called a "closed-mouth vocalization." Think of a dove cooing or perhaps an ostrich doing this grunting thing. As the scientists explain in the journal Evolution, per UT News, the sound actually comes out of the neck area after air is pushed through an esophageal pouch. In fact, the animal's mouth is shut the whole time. Lots of birds do this today, and because birds descended from dinosaurs, scientists suspect they may have communicated in a similar way. The fossil record can't prove it, though the Washington Post notes that previous research meshes with the idea, including the belief that dinosaurs had air sacs instead of vocal chords. “A cool thing about this work is the demonstration that closed-mouth behavior evolved many times,” says the lead author. “That suggests it can emerge fairly easily and be incorporated into mating displays" among different types of animals. A co-author says the study gives the impression of "a very different Jurassic world. Not only were dinosaurs feathered, but they may have had bulging necks and made booming, closed-mouth sounds." But if so, the makers of Jurassic Park didn't get it all wrong: The hiss of the film's velociraptors came from a goose, per Vulture. (Scientists have grown chickens with dinosaur legs.)

answers: Sen. Elizabeth Warren went after Wells Fargo CEO John Stumpf during a Senate hearing Tuesday with the kind of heat she usually reserves for Donald Trump tweetstorms. pred: Vanderbilt University has announced that it will pay $1.2 million to remove the name "Confederate" from one of its campus dormitories, Confederate Memorial Hall, after a 14-year effort to rename the building. The university has referred to the dorm as Memorial Hall since 2002, but a court ruling prevented them from making the change due to a $50,000 gift from the United Daughters of the Confederacy in 193. Chancellor Nicholas Zeppos believes the move is necessary to "move Vanderbilt forward" and address the "symbol and reminder of racism, slavery, and a very bloody Civil War" that the building represents. The decision comes as universities across the country are re-evaluating their building names and grappling with issues of diversity and inclusivity. In Nashville, Middle Tennessee State University is also considering removing the name of Confederate General Nathan Bedford Forrest from one of its buildings, and the Tennessee Board of Regents has approved the change. answers: In an attempt to bury a relic of its past, Vanderbilt University announced Monday that it will pay more than $1 million to remove the word "Confederate" from one of its dorms, the Tennessean reports. Chancellor Nicholas S. Zeppos called the inscription on Confederate Memorial Hall "a reminder of racism, slavery and a very, very bloody Civil War." The Nashville university's efforts to change the name in 2002 were halted when the United Daughters of the Confederacy sued. The group’s $50,000 donation in 1933 helped build the dorm. A state appeals court ruled the building could be renamed Memorial Hall only after Vanderbilt gave the United Daughters back their money—$1.2 million in today’s dollars. The university has the cash, thanks to a raft of anonymous contributors who wanted the tie to America’s painful past broken for good. "It's a symbol that is, for many people, deeply offensive and painful," Zeppos told the Tennessean. "And to walk past it or to have to live in that space is really something that I just don't think is acceptable.” Yet tampering with Confederate imagery remains controversial, and a new state law makes it harder to do so, the AP reports. Efforts by Middle Tennessee State University in Murfreesboro to remove the name of Confederate Gen. Nathan Bedford Forrest from a building could face a tougher climb under the Tennessee Heritage Protection Act, which now requires a vote of two-thirds of the state’s Historical Commission rather than a simple majority.

pred: The recent disappearance of Malaysia Airlines Flight 37 has sparked a renewed interest in the use of Bayesian statistical methods in search and rescue operations. Bayesian techniques, which were used to locate the wreckage of Air France Flight 47 in 201, are being applied to the current search for MH370. These methods involve using statistical tools to update probabilities and estimate the location of the plane based on new information and data. The approach involves dividing the search area into a grid and assigning probabilities to each cell, taking into account factors such as the likelihood of different causes of the disappearance, historical data from previous crashes, and the results of previous searches. The Bayesian approach allows for the consideration of contradictory theories and the ability to update probabilities as new information becomes available. While some experts have expressed skepticism about the use of Bayesian methods in this case, advocates argue that they provide a valuable framework for refining subjective judgment and adapting to changing information. The Malaysian government has not confirmed whether they are using Bayesian techniques in the search, but a team of French investigators with expertise in these methods is involved. In the case of Air France Flight 47, Bayesian statistics helped narrow the search area and ultimately locate the wreckage, solving the mystery of the crash. However, finding debris does not necessarily mean finding the bulk of the plane, and even if it is located, it may not provide many clues about the fate of the flight.
answers: Forget satellite images and aerial searches—the best way to find Malaysia Airlines Flight MH370 may be with mathematical techniques dating back to the 18th century, the BBC reports. That's how Air France flight 447 was found in 2009, using "Bayesian statistics" to measure the probability of the plane being in one place or another. Named after Presbyterian minister and mathematician Thomas Bayes, the technique allowed experts to apply several factors to each point on a map: For example, what was the chance it crashed from mechanical failure? How far do planes tend to crash from their last known location? What was the chance that search teams missed debris in various locations? It's like picking a restaurant by balancing how full it is, what your favorite restaurant-review website says, and so on—except that experts hunting for Airbus A330 did that for each point where plane may have crashed in the Atlantic, Five Thirty-Eight notes. It was so hard that the US team of statisticians invited by France gave up, until they de-emphasized one statistic: that a plane's black box emits a signal after a crash 90% of the time. They changed their findings, and presto, the plane was found. Bayesian techniques have helped people find World War II U-boats, men overboard, and sunken treasure, but there's no evidence that Malaysia is employing them now. "I suspect that they just guess, like professional baseball managers used to do before Moneyball," says a biostatistician.

pred: In a recent development in the trial of Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the lawyers for the Boston Marathon bomber have filed a motion for a new trial, citing that the widespread outrage and publicity surrounding the 2013 attack made it impossible for him to receive a fair trial in Boston. The lawyers argue that even jurors would have seen friends and relatives posting about the bombing on social media, and a recent Supreme Court ruling has thrown into question the definition of "crime of violence" used to convict Tsarnaev. The appeal also notes that only three of the 34 prisoners sentenced to federal death row in the past 50 years have been executed, and the process is expected to take years to play out. Meanwhile, Tsarnaev has become the 62nd inmate on federal death row, with his fate likely to be determined through a lengthy appeals process. The federal government's use of the death penalty is seen as unusual compared to state-level executions, with most federal death row inmates being sentenced for crimes committed in specific states rather than having a national logic. The paradox of federal death row is that while it is the responsibility of the US government, the application of capital punishment has been more unpredictable and varied than in state-level systems.
answers: Not a huge surprise: Lawyers for Boston marathon bomber Dzhokhar Tsarnaev today appealed his death-penalty conviction, arguing that "continuous and unrelenting publicity" biased jurors and made a fair trial in Boston impossible, NBC News reports. "Put simply, prejudicial media coverage, events, and environment saturated greater Boston, including the social networks of actual trial jurors, and made it an improper venue for the trial of this case," say the papers. The lawyers want another trial at a new location, the AP reports. Earlier this year, the Guardian reminded us that Tsarnaev's appeals will likely delay his punishment for "at least a decade." The April 15, 2013, bomb strike in Boston left three dead and more than 200 maimed or injured.

pred:
On August 1, 2018, a decomposing body was found inside a column at a supermarket in Lancaster, California. The body was identified as that of Raymundo Rivera, 35, according to the Los Angeles County Coroner's office. The manager of the store reported a strong smell coming from the column, which led a repairman to investigate and discover the body. The body was found in a state of decomposition, and investigators believe it may be the body of a suspect who escaped from a traffic stop earlier that night. The suspect is believed to have entered the column and gotten stuck, falling or dying inside. The Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department is leading the investigation.


On the same day, another decomposing body was found inside a column at a different supermarket in Lancaster. The store manager reported a strong smell and called a plumber to investigate, who found a shoe and leg inside the column. Investigators believe the body may be that of a suspect who escaped from law enforcement earlier in the day and ran into the store, falling or getting stuck inside the column.

Both incidents occurred at supermarkets in the 70 block of West Avenue K in Lancaster, with access doors on the roof leading to the front fascia of the store. Customers were turned away from the stores while investigations were ongoing, and authorities are encouraging anyone with information to contact the Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department or Crime Stoppers.

The discoveries of the bodies inside the columns have left investigators and witnesses in shock, with one describing it as a "strange development" and a "horrible way to die."
answers: A strong smell outside a California supermarket turned out to be someone who had decomposed in one of the store's entrance columns, ABC 7 reports. Police were called Saturday to the WinCo Foods in Lancaster after a plumber, told it was likely a septic problem, helped knock brick from the column and saw a shoe and a leg inside. "It's been over 100 degrees up here every day," says LA County Sheriff's Lt. John Corina. "I can't imagine being inside that column and just baking. It's a strange development, and if he died that way, it's a horrible way to die." Police say the victim may be a man who was stopped for a fake license plate Monday night, NBC Los Angeles reports. He crashed the vehicle, ran away from deputies, and got into the supermarket. The man "ran up the steps where the managers [are]," an eyewitness tells CBS Los Angeles. "And somehow he got on the roof. Last I heard, he got away." From up there, he could have reached the store fascia and descended into the column. Whether he died from the fall or baked inside is unclear; he was found on his feet with an arm stuck behind his head. "I saw some gooey liquid and it smelled really foul ... it was oozing out of the pillar onto the pavement," says a witness. "It smelled like death."

pred: The Academy of Motion Pictures Arts and Sciences (AMPAS) has faced criticism for its lack of diversity in recent years, particularly in regards to its predominantly older, male, and white membership. In response, the organization has taken steps to address this issue by inviting a record number of new members, with almost half of them being women and 41% being people of color. The list of invited industry professionals includes well-known Hollywood veterans, international heavyweights, and up-and-coming directors such as Catherine Breillat, Park Chan-wook, and Hou Hsiao-Hsien, as well as a diverse group of actors, including Luis Guzman, Adam Beach, Kate Beckinsale, and Idris Elba. This move comes after the hashtag #OscarsSoWhite gained traction on social media, with April Reign, a former attorney and managing editor of BroadwayBlack.com, using it to highlight the lack of diversity in Oscar nominations. The Academy has committed to doubling the number of women and people of color in its membership by 2020, starting with the release of its latest list of invitees, which is the most diverse class to date. This is a significant step towards a more inclusive and representative film industry.
answers: Apparently still smarting from some of Chris Rock's Oscars barbs, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences invited 683 people—many of them women and minorities—to join in an unprecedented move Wednesday, Reuters reports. The voting group behind the Oscars is largely old, white, and male and was lambasted this year with the hashtag #OscarsSoWhite after two years in a row of all-white acting nominees. In response, the academy is attempting to—as the AV Club puts it—" add seemingly goddamn everyone it had, for one reason or another, forgotten to invite into its membership.” The actors, directors, and others invited Wednesday include Idris Elba, Eva Mendes, Oscar Isaac, John Boyega, Ice Cube, Greta Gerqig, Michael B. Jordan, Vivica A. Fox, the Wachowskis, James Wan, Luis Guzmán, Kate Beckinsale, Park Chan-wook, James Hong, Michelle Rodriguez, and not one, not two, but three Wayans brothers. Of the new invitees, 46% are women and 41% are people of color. If all 683 accept their invite, women would account for 27% of the more than 7,000 academy members (up from 25%) and minorities would total 11% (up from 8%). “I'm especially happy to be part of such a diverse group. I actually want to hang out and watch movies with most of the people on this list," Arab-German director Lexi Alexander tells the Los Angeles Times. “To be honest, I cried a few tears when I started to get congratulation tweets in Arabic.” Other invitees took to Twitter to share similar sentiments. “Excited to use my vote to nominate talent that reflects the real world we live in—DIVERSITY," tweets Brie Larson, who won best actress at this year's Oscars.

pred: Robert Durst, a real estate heir and suspect in multiple murders, was sentenced to 7 years in prison on a federal gun charge in New Orleans. He has agreed to serve his time in California, where he faces a separate murder charge in the death of his friend Susan Berman. Durst, who is 73 years old and uses a wheelchair, pleaded guilty to the gun charge in February and expressed his innocence in Berman's killing. His attorneys have requested that he be housed at a low-security federal prison in California to accommodate his poor health. The plea agreement also resolved several pending charges against Durst in New York and Louisiana state court. Durst was arrested in March 2015 after a search of his hotel room turned up a handgun, and he has previous convictions in Texas related to jumping bail and carrying a weapon across state lines. The HBO documentary "The Jinx" chronicled investigations into Durst's past crimes, including the dismemberment of a neighbor in Texas and the disappearance of his wife in New York. In the final episode, Durst was caught on camera muttering "I killed them all," leading to renewed interest in his case. Durst's wealthy family runs 1 World Trade Center. His transfer to California is expected to happen within four to six weeks.
answers: A New Orleans judge officially approved Robert Durst's February plea deal on a gun charge and sentenced him to seven years in prison on Wednesday, Reuters reports. For more than a year, the real estate heir has languished in a New Orleans prison after being nabbed with a handgun in his hotel room. He was busted for that felony just one day before the series finale of HBO's The Jinx, in which he famously appeared to confess to three murders by saying, "What the hell did I do? Killed them all, of course." US District Judge Kurt Engelhardt also agreed that Durst can be transferred to a low-security prison with medical facilities in San Pedro, Calif., where the 73-year-old's attorneys say his "advanced age and poor health" can be better addressed, per the Advocate. In California, Durst will face a murder charge in the 2000 death of friend Susan Berman, the AP reports. (A Texas judge believes Durst once dumped a cat's head on her doorstep.)

pred: There are several updates in the ongoing trial of Jesse Matthew Jr., who is charged with the murder of 18-year-old University of Virginia student Hannah Graham in 2014. In court hearings, new details have emerged regarding the investigation and evidence collected by law enforcement. A judge heard evidence in the case and granted a motion to question detectives involved in the initial investigation, including one who used a bloodhound to track Graham's scent. The bloodhound detected her scent at various locations, including an industrial site near downtown Charlottesville, her apartment, and the passenger door of Matthew's car. The detective testified that the dog's findings provided probable cause for the search warrant that was used to obtain Matthew's apartment and car. The defense challenged the search warrant, arguing that the evidence was inconsistent with witness statements and video surveillance, but the judge ruled that there was enough probable cause to justify the search. Additionally, a former detective testified that the bloodhound found traces of Graham's blood at Matthew's apartment and on his car. Matthew is also facing charges in the death of another student, Morgan Harrington, and is currently serving a life sentence for a previous crime. The trial is ongoing and updates will be provided by ABC 13.
answers: A former police bloodhound handler told a court on Monday that during the search for UVa student Hannah Graham, the animal detected her scent outside the car and apartment of suspect Jesse Matthew—and the scent of "fear and adrenaline" where she was allegedly attacked. The handler was testifying during a pretrial hearing where lawyers for Matthew, who is already serving three life terms in prison for a 2005 sexual assault, unsuccessfully challenged the search warrants police obtained for Matthew's home and vehicle, the AP reports. He said the 7-year-old dog, Shaker, traced Graham's scent from downtown Charlottesville to a mulch pile at an industrial site, where the smell of fear was strongest. Matthew, 33, faces a capital murder charge in the 2014 death of Graham, whose body was found on an abandoned property around six weeks after she disappeared. He has also been charged with the 2009 murder of a Virginia Tech student. At Monday's hearing, another police witness testified that, contrary to what the defense claimed, the scent trail was consistent with surveillance videos that showed Graham wandering around, reports WSET. The officer shared text message records indicating that she was lost the night she disappeared.The Washington Post notes that Matthew is seen walking alongside Graham in some of the surveillance videos.

pred: On March 21, 2012, soldiers stormed the presidential palace in Mali and overthrew the government, just one month before President Amadou Toumani Toure was set to step down at the end of his legal term. The mutiny was sparked by the government's handling of an ethnic Tuareg insurgency in the country's north, which has resulted in the deaths of numerous soldiers and the displacement of tens of thousands of people. The mutineers, who are frustrated with the government's inability to combat the rebellion, have suspended the constitution and dissolved institutions, and have imposed a nationwide curfew. The West African regional bloc ECOWAS has condemned the coup, calling it a "reprehensible" and "misguided" act. The mutineers, who are now in control of the presidential palace, have announced the formation of a National Committee for the Reestablishment of Democracy and the Restoration of the State, with the goal of returning power to a democratically elected president as soon as possible. The whereabouts of President Toure are unknown, and it is unclear whether he is in a safe location or has been arrested. The coup has been met with celebration from some residents of Bamako, who are firing in the air in support of the mutiny, while others are concerned about the stability of the country. The situation remains unclear, with reports of gunfire continuing into the night and the government refusing to postpone an upcoming presidential election. The Tuareg rebellion, which began in mid-January, has fueled tensions in the country and led to the return of Tuareg fighters from Libya, who are now fighting for a homeland in the country's northern desert. The government has not disclosed the number of soldiers killed in the fighting.
answers: A military coup is under way in Mali, as renegade soldiers stormed the presidential palace in the capital of Bamako today, and soon afterward appeared on state TV to announce that they had booted the president and taken over the country, the BBC reports. The soldiers said they were suspending the constitution, dissolving its institutions and instituting a nationwide curfew. They said they were taking power because of President Amadou Toumani Toure's failure to defeat a rebellion by the Taureg tribes in the north, the AP reports. The military “has decided to assume its responsibilities and end the incompetent and disavowed regime of Amadou Toumani Toure,” the troops said, adding that it “does not in any way aim to confiscate power, and we solemnly swear to return power to a democratically elected president as soon as national unity and territorial integrity are established.” The mutiny comes just a month before Toure was set to leave office legally.

pred: Last week, a fatal accident involving a Tesla vehicle occurred while the car was in Autopilot mode, killing the driver. Tesla has been working to establish the facts of the incident and has released a statement expressing their condolences to the family and friends affected. According to the company, the driver had received visual and audible warnings before the collision, but their hands were not detected on the wheel for six seconds prior to the impact. The accident is believed to have been caused by a previously damaged crash attenuator, which had not been repaired after a previous accident. The family of the driver has come forward to reveal that he had complained to the dealer about the Autopilot system veering towards the same barrier on multiple occasions before the fatal crash. The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) is still investigating the incident. Separately, a 38-year-old man was killed when his Tesla crashed and caught fire in Mountain View, California, and his family has claimed that he had complained to the dealer about the same issue before the accident. The California Department of Transportation (CalTrans) has stated that replacement work on the damaged barrier was scheduled, but storms may have delayed the repair. The incident has raised questions about the safety of autonomous vehicles and the responsibility of manufacturers to ensure the safety of their technology. Tesla has defended its Autopilot system, stating that it makes vehicles safer for occupants, pedestrians, and cyclists, but acknowledged that it is not a guarantee against accidents.
answers: The Tesla Model X that slammed into a highway barrier in California last week and killed the 38-year-old Apple engineer at the wheel had been on autopilot at the time of the crash, Tesla confirms in a blog post. But the company says driver Walter Huang "received several visual and one audible hands-on warning earlier in the drive," adding that "the driver’s hands were not detected on the wheel for six seconds prior to the collision." Tesla also laid some blame on state highway officials, asserting that "the reason this crash was so severe is because the crash attenuator, a highway safety barrier which is designed to reduce the impact into a concrete lane divider, had been crushed in a prior accident without being replaced." The fact that the vehicle was on autopilot is likely to be controversial because, as ABC News notes, driver Huang had reportedly been concerned about it. His brother told KGO that Huang had brought his Model X back to the dealership complaining that when he used the feature, the car kept swerving toward the very same barrier that it eventually struck in the fatal collision. The NTSB is investigating the crash, including the fire that broke out after the collision. The accident follows another in Arizona in which a self-driving Uber struck and killed a pedestrian.

pred: There are several ongoing investigations and controversies surrounding the Trump administration's potential ties to Russia, with the latest news focusing on a classified memo written by Republicans on the House Intelligence Committee that alleges misconduct by senior FBI officials involved in the Russia probe. answers: It's all up to President Trump now. Democrats are fuming over the decision. Trump now has five days to decide whether to allow the memo to be released; his approval is necessary because it's a classified document. pred: A murder suspect, Jayveon Caballero, remains at large following a shooting in Montpelier, Vermont that left a former member of the Vermont Frost Heaves basketball team dead. The victim, Markus Austin, was shot outside his apartment early Sunday morning, and police have learned that he and Caballero may have been friends and were involved in an altercation earlier that night at a bar in Barre. Caballero, 29, is wanted for second-degree murder and an arrest warrant has been issued with bail set at $50,000. However, police believe he may have fled the state and are unsure if he is traveling by vehicle. Caballero's girlfriend, Desiree Cary, 2, was taken into custody on unrelated drug charges but is believed to have been involved in the altercation at the bar that led to the shooting. The shooting is the first murder in Montpelier in almost a century, according to police, and the city has a population of around 7,5000. Authorities are asking for anyone with information on Caballero's whereabouts to contact them.
answers: Police in the country's smallest state capital are investigating something that hasn't happened there in living memory: a murder. Police in Vermont say they have issued an arrest warrant for 29-year-old Jayveon Caballero, who is accused of fatally shooting Markus Austin in Montpelier early Sunday after an altercation, the Times Argus reports. Witnesses told investigators that Austin, 33, assaulted Caballero’s girlfriend, Desiree Cary, during the altercation outside a bar in the nearby city of Barre early Sunday. Police say Caballero later confronted Austin outside his apartment and shot him dead. The body was found in the parking lot of Austin's apartment complex. A Vermont State Police spokesman says Caballero, who knew Austin before the shooting, fled the town and probably the state after the shooting, WCAX reports. The police spokesman says Caballero is believed to have boarded a Greyhound bus bound for New York City on Sunday morning, which made numerous stops in other states along the way. Later Sunday, Cary, 22, was arrested on apparently unrelated charges of selling heroin and crack cocaine. With around 7,500 residents, Montpelier has the smallest population of any state capital, the AP notes. Police Chief Anthony Facos says this is the city's first murder since a woman shot her husband in the 1920s.

pred: Sony held an event today and unveiled its next-generation gaming console, the PlayStation 4 (PS4), which is designed to provide a more personalized and mobile gaming experience. The console will also offer social features such as the ability to quickly upload gameplay videos and spectate other players. While the graphics are improved, they are not a huge leap forward from the current generation, with some demos looking similar to those seen on the PS3 and Xbox 360. However, the PS4 will have native streaming capabilities and the ability to play games from the cloud, as well as support for streaming video apps like Netflix and Hulu. The console is expected to be available in stores this holiday season, but the price and design are still unknown. Additionally, the PS4 will have backwards compatibility with the PS3, but no word on native compatibility. Sony also announced that it will integrate Facebook into the console to connect with the PlayStation Network and offer personalized recommendations for games based on user habits.
answers: Sony today unveiled its next-generation gaming system, PlayStation 4, saying the console will be part of a new ecosystem focused on hardware, software, and "the fastest, most powerful gaming network." The new console is the Japanese electronics giant's first major game machine since the PlayStation 3 went on sale in 2006. Today's unveiling is Sony's attempt to steal the spotlight, at least until Microsoft unveils its next Xbox in June at the E3 video game expo in Los Angeles. Among the PS4's revisions is an updated controller that adds a touchpad, motion control, and a "share" button. The controller also features a light bar, which means a new PlayStation camera can more easily track the device. Sony has struggled lately to keep up with Microsoft and other rivals such as Apple and Samsung. The company is promising nifty mobile devices, sophisticated digital cameras, and other gadgetry as part of its comeback effort. More details at Gizmodo and TechCrunch.

pred: The latest news from Syria indicates that the situation in the country remains tense and violent, with ongoing protests and crackdowns by the government against anti-government demonstrators. The US Ambassador to Syria, Robert Ford, visited the embattled town of Hama on Thursday to show support for the Syrians fighting for democracy, meeting with residents and visiting a hospital where wounded protesters were being treated. The Syrian government accused the US of "interfering" in its affairs and claimed that Ford's visit was "evidence of US involvement in the ongoing events in Syria." Despite this, Ford plans to stay in the area for upcoming protests planned for Friday. Reports from activists suggest that the largest protest yet in Syria took place in Hama, with over 50,0000 people participating, resulting in the deaths of 13 protesters across the country, including five in Homs and six in Dumair. The city of Hama has been a symbol of opposition since a 1982 crackdown by the government and has seen continued violence in recent weeks, with an estimated 20,000 people killed. Diplomatic efforts are also underway, with the Syrian ambassador summoned to meet with US officials to express concerns about embassy staff filming protests in the US.
answers: America's ambassador to Syria visited one of the cities worst-hit by the crackdown against anti-government protesters yesterday, reports CNN. Robert Ford went to the city of Hama "to make absolutely clear with his physical presence that we stand with those Syrians who are expressing their right to speak for change," an embassy spokeswoman says. An estimated 500,000 people joined an anti-government rally in the city last Friday. The Syrian government accused Ford of going to the city without authorization, saying the visit is "clear evidence of the US involvement in the ongoing events in Syria and its bids to aggravate the situations which destabilize Syria.'' An embassy spokeswoman says the Syrian government was told a delegation would be visiting Hama, although no Syrian "handlers" were taken and officials weren't informed about the ambassador's presence. The city has been a symbol of opposition since 1982, when a crackdown ordered by the present Syrian leader's father killed around 20,000 people, al-Jazeera notes.

pred:
